questions.stem: A data engineer wants to use Auto Loader to ingest JSON files that arrive continuously in an S3 bucket. They need to ensure the schema is inferred automatically and stored durably so that schema changes in new files can be detected. Which option MUST be set when configuring Auto Loader for schema inference?

options.text: `cloudFiles.inferColumnTypes`
`cloudFiles.schemaLocation`
`cloudFiles.schemaHints`
`cloudFiles.maxFilesPerTrigger`

questions.explanation: `cloudFiles.schemaLocation` is required when using Auto Loader schema inference. It specifies a cloud storage path where Auto Loader persists the inferred schema across runs. Without it, Auto Loader cannot store the schema between micro-batches, so schema inference would fail or reset. When the option is set, Auto Loader compares newly inferred schemas against the stored schema to detect evolution. Option A (`inferColumnTypes`) controls whether columns are inferred as typed or all-string, but it is not required. Option C (`schemaHints`) lets you manually specify types for select columns but is optional. Option D (`maxFilesPerTrigger`) controls throughput, not schema inference.
questions.reference: Auto Loader schema inference and cloudFiles.schemaLocation

questions.stem: Which Structured Streaming trigger causes a job to process all available data in the source exactly once and then terminate the stream, behaving similarly to a batch job but retaining stream semantics like checkpointing?

options.text: `trigger(processingTime='0 seconds')`
`trigger(once=True)`
`trigger(availableNow=True)`
`trigger(continuous='1 second')`

questions.explanation: `trigger(availableNow=True)` is the modern replacement for `trigger(once=True)`. It processes all data available at the time the stream starts across multiple micro-batches (exploiting parallelism), then shuts down the stream. It retains full streaming semantics including checkpoints and exactly-once guarantees. `trigger(once=True)` also terminates after processing available data but processes everything in a single micro-batch, which can be slow for large backlogs. `processingTime='0 seconds'` runs as fast as possible but keeps the stream alive. `continuous` mode is for ultra-low-latency processing with millisecond latency but limited operator support.
questions.reference: Structured Streaming trigger modes: availableNow vs once

options.text: `gold_revenue` is a Streaming Live Table; it incrementally aggregates new rows appended to `silver_orders`
`gold_revenue` is a Materialized View (Live Table); it uses `dlt.read()` so it performs a full recomputation of the aggregation on each pipeline run
`gold_revenue` is a View; it does not persist data and is computed on every query
`gold_revenue` will fail at runtime because `dlt.read()` cannot be used on a Streaming Live Table

questions.explanation: `dlt.read()` (not `dlt.read_stream()`) makes `gold_revenue` a materialized Live Table, also called a Materialized View in DLT terminology. Because it uses a static read (not streaming), DLT must recompute the full aggregation on every pipeline run — it cannot do incremental updates. This is appropriate for aggregations like revenue totals where correctness requires seeing all data. `dlt.read_stream()` would create a Streaming Live Table, which processes incrementally. Option A is wrong because `dlt.read()` (not `dlt.read_stream()`) was used. Option C is wrong because `@dlt.table` persists data (use `@dlt.view` for non-persisted views). Option D is wrong — `dlt.read()` on a streaming table reads the current materialized state, which is valid.
questions.reference: DLT: LIVE TABLE vs STREAMING LIVE TABLE, dlt.read() vs dlt.read_stream()

questions.stem:
An e-commerce company streams clickstream events to a Delta table. A downstream Structured Streaming job joins `clickstream` events with `product_catalog` (a static Delta table that updates daily). The data engineer uses the following pattern:

```python
clicks = spark.readStream.table('clickstream')
catalog = spark.read.table('product_catalog')  # static read
result = clicks.join(catalog, 'product_id')
```

What is a CRITICAL limitation of this approach?

options.text: Joining a stream with a static DataFrame is not supported in Structured Streaming
The `product_catalog` snapshot is loaded once at stream startup and will NOT reflect daily updates to the catalog without restarting the stream
The join will fail because `product_id` must be a watermarked column when joining streams with static data
The static DataFrame is broadcast automatically, which will cause OOM errors on large catalogs

questions.explanation: Stream-to-static joins are supported in Structured Streaming, but the static DataFrame is evaluated (snapshotted) once at the start of the stream and held in memory for the lifetime of the stream. Daily updates to `product_catalog` will NOT be reflected in the join until the stream is restarted. This is a real operational limitation. Solutions: restart the stream daily, use a streaming join with `foreachBatch` and re-read the catalog inside the batch function, or use Delta Change Data Feed to stream catalog changes. Option A is incorrect — stream-static joins ARE supported. Option C is incorrect — watermarks are needed for stream-stream joins, not stream-static. Option D may be a concern for large catalogs but is not the critical correctness issue described.
questions.reference: Structured Streaming stream-static joins, static DataFrame limitations

questions.stem: A data engineer needs to perform a CDC MERGE into a Silver Delta table using the following logic: update existing rows, insert new rows, and delete rows that no longer exist in the source. The source is a daily full snapshot. Which MERGE statement CORRECTLY implements this logic?

options.text: `MERGE INTO silver USING source ON silver.id = source.id WHEN MATCHED THEN UPDATE SET * WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN INSERT *`
`MERGE INTO silver USING source ON silver.id = source.id WHEN MATCHED THEN UPDATE SET * WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN INSERT * WHEN NOT MATCHED BY SOURCE THEN DELETE`
`MERGE INTO silver USING source ON silver.id = source.id WHEN NOT MATCHED BY TARGET THEN INSERT * WHEN NOT MATCHED BY SOURCE THEN DELETE`
`MERGE INTO silver USING source ON silver.id = source.id WHEN MATCHED THEN DELETE WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN INSERT *`

questions.explanation: When the source is a full daily snapshot, rows missing from the source should be deleted from the target. Option B correctly handles all three cases: (1) `WHEN MATCHED THEN UPDATE SET *` — row exists in both source and target: update it, (2) `WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN INSERT *` — row is in source but not target: insert it, (3) `WHEN NOT MATCHED BY SOURCE THEN DELETE` — row is in target but not in source: delete it. This is the full-sync MERGE pattern. Option A is missing the delete clause. Option C is missing the UPDATE for matched rows. Option D deletes matched rows instead of updating them — this would delete every row that exists in both tables.
questions.reference: Delta Lake MERGE INTO with WHEN NOT MATCHED BY SOURCE

questions.stem: A data engineer is configuring Auto Loader with file notification mode on Azure. Select TWO statements that are TRUE about file notification mode compared to directory listing mode.

options.text: File notification mode uses Azure Event Grid and Azure Queue Storage to receive push notifications when files are created, eliminating the need to list the entire directory
File notification mode requires no additional Azure infrastructure and works out-of-the-box with any storage account
File notification mode scales better than directory listing mode for buckets with millions of files because it avoids repeated full directory scans
File notification mode is the default mode when using `format('cloudFiles')`
File notification mode can miss files if the notification queue is purged before Auto Loader processes them

questions.explanation: Option A is correct: on Azure, file notification mode integrates with Azure Event Grid (to detect new blobs) and Azure Queue Storage (as the message queue) — Auto Loader sets these up automatically with appropriate permissions, but the Azure resources are required. Option C is correct: the primary benefit of file notification mode is scale — for buckets with millions of files, listing the directory on every trigger is expensive (O(n) in total files); notification mode is event-driven and O(new files). Option B is wrong — it requires Azure Event Grid + Queue Storage to be provisioned. Option D is wrong — directory listing is the default. Option E is technically a concern but not a standard documented behavior of the service; the notification queue is generally reliable and Auto Loader does not rely solely on notifications (it has idempotency mechanisms).
questions.reference: Auto Loader file notification mode vs directory listing mode

questions.stem: In a Delta Live Tables pipeline, a data engineer wants to track records that fail a data quality check WITHOUT stopping the pipeline and WITHOUT dropping the failed records. Which DLT expectation decorator achieves this?

options.text: `@dlt.expect_or_fail`
`@dlt.expect_or_drop`
`@dlt.expect`
`@dlt.expect_all`

questions.explanation: `@dlt.expect` (or `@dlt.expect_all` for multiple constraints) records violations in the DLT event log and includes failed records in the output table — the pipeline continues and no data is dropped. This is the 'warn and continue' behavior. Compare: `@dlt.expect_or_drop` drops violating records and continues; `@dlt.expect_or_fail` halts the pipeline on any violation. `@dlt.expect` is ideal when you want visibility into data quality issues without interrupting the pipeline or losing data. The violation counts are surfaced in the DLT pipeline UI and event log. Option A stops the pipeline. Option B drops records. Option D (`@dlt.expect_all`) applies multiple constraints but still uses the same 'warn' behavior — it is the correct multi-constraint version of `@dlt.expect`, but `@dlt.expect` is the specific single-constraint answer here.
questions.reference: DLT data quality: @dlt.expect vs @dlt.expect_or_drop vs @dlt.expect_or_fail

questions.stem: A retail company implements a DLT pipeline using `APPLY CHANGES INTO` for CDC processing from a source that sends INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE events. The CDC source sometimes sends duplicate events with the same sequence number for the same key. How does `APPLY CHANGES INTO` handle these duplicates?

options.text: `APPLY CHANGES INTO` has no deduplication; all duplicates are written to the target table

`APPLY CHANGES INTO` deduplicates events based on the sequence column — only the event with the highest sequence number for each key is applied; ties are resolved by retaining the most recently received event
`APPLY CHANGES INTO` deduplicates based on the sequence column and ignores any duplicate event with the same key and sequence number
`APPLY CHANGES INTO` fails and raises an error when duplicate sequence numbers are detected

questions.explanation: `APPLY CHANGES INTO` uses the `SEQUENCE BY` column to determine the correct ordering of CDC events and handles duplicates: if two events arrive with the same primary key AND the same sequence value, the second occurrence is treated as a duplicate and ignored. This is critical for exactly-once CDC semantics when source systems may replay events. The engine applies only the event with the highest sequence number per key. Option A is wrong — APPLY CHANGES INTO has built-in deduplication. Option B is partially correct about sequence-based ordering but wrong about 'ties' — duplicates (same key + same sequence) are idempotently dropped, not resolved by arrival order. Option D is wrong — duplicates do not cause failures.
questions.reference: DLT APPLY CHANGES INTO sequence handling and deduplication

questions.stem:
A data engineer reads CDF (Change Data Feed) data from a Delta table using:

```python
df = spark.read.format('delta') \
    .option('readChangeFeed', 'true') \
    .option('startingVersion', 5) \
    .table('inventory')
```

What additional column uniquely identifies whether a row represents a before-update value or an after-update value?

options.text: `_commit_version`
`_change_type`
`_operation`
`_row_version`

questions.explanation: The `_change_type` column in CDF output distinguishes the type of change for each row. For an UPDATE, two rows are emitted per changed row: one with `_change_type = 'update_preimage'` (the before-update values) and one with `_change_type = 'update_postimage'` (the after-update values). Other values include `'insert'` and `'delete'`. `_commit_version` tells you which transaction the change belongs to but doesn't distinguish pre/post-image. `_operation` and `_row_version` are not standard CDF columns. The full set of CDF metadata columns is: `_change_type`, `_commit_version`, and `_commit_timestamp`.
questions.reference: Delta Lake Change Data Feed: _change_type values

questions.stem: In a Medallion architecture, a data engineer stores raw JSON event data exactly as received from a Kafka topic, without any transformation or schema enforcement. Which layer does this data belong to?

options.text: Gold layer
Silver layer
Bronze layer
Staging layer

questions.explanation: The Bronze layer is the raw landing zone for data in a Medallion architecture. It stores data in its original format — no schema enforcement, no type casting, no deduplication. The goal is fidelity: capture everything exactly as it arrived. Storing raw Kafka JSON events directly into Bronze Delta tables is the canonical Bronze pattern (often using Auto Loader or a Kafka streaming connector). The Silver layer applies cleaning, typing, and deduplication. The Gold layer applies business aggregations. 'Staging' is not a standard Medallion layer name. Option A (Gold) is aggregated/business-ready. Option B (Silver) is cleansed and typed.
questions.reference: Medallion architecture: Bronze layer purpose

questions.stem: A data engineer attempts to write a DataFrame to an existing Delta table that has a different schema — the incoming DataFrame has an extra column `discount_pct` not present in the table. The write uses the default options. What happens?

options.text: The write succeeds; Delta silently ignores the extra column
The write fails with a schema mismatch error unless `mergeSchema` or `overwriteSchema` is enabled
The write succeeds and the `discount_pct` column is automatically added to the table schema
The write fails because Delta tables are immutable and cannot accept new data after creation

questions.explanation: By default, Delta Lake enforces schema on write — a write operation that presents columns not in the existing table schema will fail with an `AnalysisException` (schema mismatch). To allow new columns, you must either: (1) add `.option('mergeSchema', 'true')` to the write operation (adds the new column to the table schema), or (2) set `spark.databricks.delta.schema.autoMerge.enabled = true` at the session level. `overwriteSchema` is used when you want to replace the entire schema (usually with `mode('overwrite')`). Option A is wrong — Delta does not silently drop columns. Option C describes the behavior when mergeSchema IS enabled. Option D is completely wrong — Delta tables accept new data by design.
questions.reference: Delta Lake schema enforcement and schema evolution with mergeSchema

questions.stem: A data engineer implements SCD Type 2 using a `MERGE INTO` statement on a Delta table. The table has columns: `customer_id`, `email`, `is_current` (BOOLEAN), `effective_date`, `end_date`. When a customer updates their email, which MERGE clause pattern CORRECTLY implements the SCD2 logic?

options.text: `WHEN MATCHED AND target.is_current = true AND target.email != source.email THEN UPDATE SET is_current = false, end_date = current_date()`
`WHEN MATCHED THEN UPDATE SET email = source.email, effective_date = current_date()`
`WHEN MATCHED THEN DELETE`
`WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN INSERT (customer_id, email, is_current, effective_date, end_date) VALUES (source.customer_id, source.email, true, current_date(), null)`

questions.explanation: SCD Type 2 with MERGE requires TWO operations: (1) Close the old record by setting `is_current = false` and `end_date = current_date()` — this is the MATCHED clause in option A. (2) Insert a new record with the updated email, `is_current = true`, `effective_date = current_date()`, `end_date = NULL` — this requires a separate INSERT (either in the same MERGE using `WHEN NOT MATCHED BY SOURCE` logic or via a subsequent INSERT). Option A correctly implements step 1. Option B is SCD Type 1 (overwrites history by updating in place). Option C deletes the record — loses history entirely. Option D is the new row INSERT step which is also needed, but alone without closing the old record it creates duplicate 'current' rows. The correct full MERGE has both A and D logic, but A best describes the critical SCD2-specific action.
questions.reference: SCD Type 2 MERGE pattern: closing current records

questions.stem: A data engineering team wants to enable Change Data Feed on an existing Delta table called `customer_profiles`. Which statement CORRECTLY enables CDF?

options.text: `ALTER TABLE customer_profiles SET TBLPROPERTIES (delta.enableChangeDataFeed = true)`
`ALTER TABLE customer_profiles ENABLE CHANGE DATA FEED`
`CREATE TABLE customer_profiles TBLPROPERTIES (enableChangeDataFeed = true)`
`OPTIMIZE customer_profiles WITH CHANGE DATA FEED ENABLED`

questions.explanation: Change Data Feed is enabled on an existing Delta table using `ALTER TABLE ... SET TBLPROPERTIES (delta.enableChangeDataFeed = true)`. The property key must be exactly `delta.enableChangeDataFeed` (with the `delta.` prefix). CDF only captures changes made AFTER it is enabled — historical data before enabling is not in the CDF. For new tables, you can set this in the CREATE TABLE statement with the same TBLPROPERTIES syntax. Option B is not valid SQL syntax for Databricks. Option C uses CREATE TABLE (for existing tables you need ALTER TABLE) and omits the `delta.` prefix. Option D is not valid syntax — OPTIMIZE has nothing to do with enabling CDF.
questions.reference: Enabling Delta Lake Change Data Feed with TBLPROPERTIES

questions.stem: A data engineer is deciding whether to partition a large Delta table by `country_code`. The table has 50 million rows, receives 10,000 new rows per day, and `country_code` has 200 distinct values. Select TWO factors that would make partitioning by `country_code` a GOOD choice.

options.text: Most queries filter by `country_code`, enabling partition pruning to skip irrelevant data files
The table receives only 10,000 rows per day, which ensures each partition file is close to the 1GB target size
200 distinct values is a low-to-medium cardinality that creates a manageable number of partitions
`country_code` is a string column, which is required for Delta Lake partitioning
Each of the 200 country partitions will accumulate significant data over time, making file sizes large enough to benefit from partitioning

questions.explanation: Option A is the primary benefit of partitioning: if queries commonly filter by `country_code`, Spark can skip entire partitions (directories) at the file-system level before even reading data — this is partition pruning, which is faster than data skipping because it avoids reading files entirely. Option E is important: partitioning is beneficial when partition sizes are large (ideally GB-scale). With 50M rows and growth over time, each of the 200 country partitions could accumulate hundreds of thousands of rows — making the partition sizes substantial enough to justify the overhead. Option B is actually a concern, not a benefit: 10,000 rows/day across 200 countries is only 50 rows per country per day — very small per-partition daily writes that would create tiny files. Option C (200 values) is reasonable cardinality, but on its own doesn't justify partitioning. Option D is wrong — string is fine, but numeric types are also valid partition keys.
questions.reference: Delta Lake partitioning best practices, partition pruning

questions.stem:
A data engineer sets the following Delta table property:

```sql
ALTER TABLE events SET TBLPROPERTIES (
  'delta.logRetentionDuration' = 'interval 30 days'
);
```

What does this property control?

options.text: How long the Parquet data files are retained before VACUUM can delete them
How long the transaction log JSON files are retained, controlling the range of versions available for Time Travel
How long the table data is cached in the Databricks cluster's memory
How long audit events for this table are stored in Unity Catalog

questions.explanation: `delta.logRetentionDuration` controls how long Delta keeps the transaction log files (the JSON and checkpoint files in `_delta_log`). A longer retention means more historical versions are available for Time Travel queries. The default is 30 days. Importantly, this is distinct from `delta.deletedFileRetentionDuration`, which controls how long the actual Parquet data files are retained before VACUUM can clean them up. Both must be set appropriately to support Time Travel: the log must have the version entry AND the data files must still exist. Option A describes `delta.deletedFileRetentionDuration`. Option C describes Databricks caching (unrelated to table properties). Option D describes Unity Catalog audit logging (a separate system).
questions.reference: Delta table properties: logRetentionDuration vs deletedFileRetentionDuration

questions.stem: In Unity Catalog, what is the correct three-level namespace format for fully qualifying a table reference?

options.text: `workspace.database.table`
`catalog.schema.table`
`catalog.database.table`
`metastore.catalog.table`

questions.explanation: Unity Catalog uses a three-level namespace: `catalog.schema.table`. The levels are: (1) Catalog — the top-level container, analogous to a database in other systems; (2) Schema — equivalent to a traditional database/schema, containing tables, views, and functions; (3) Table — the individual data object. Example: `main.sales.transactions`. This replaces the two-level namespace (`database.table`) used in the legacy Hive metastore. Option A uses 'workspace' which is not a namespace level. Option C uses 'database' instead of 'schema' — Unity Catalog uses 'schema'. Option D uses 'metastore' which is the Unity Catalog infrastructure itself, not part of the namespace.
questions.reference: Unity Catalog three-level namespace: catalog.schema.table

questions.stem: A data engineer needs to grant an external data science team access to read Parquet files stored in an Azure Data Lake Storage Gen2 container, without granting them access to any other storage location. The organization uses Unity Catalog. Which sequence of steps is CORRECT?

options.text: Create an External Location referencing the container path and storage credential, then GRANT READ FILES on the External Location to the data science group
Create a Storage Credential pointing to the container path, then GRANT READ FILES on the Storage Credential
Create an External Location, then GRANT USE CATALOG on the External Location
Share the Azure service principal credentials directly with the data science team via dbutils.secrets

questions.explanation: Unity Catalog external storage access has two components: (1) Storage Credential — wraps the cloud identity (Azure service principal or managed identity) that has cloud-level access to storage; (2) External Location — combines a Storage Credential with a specific cloud storage path (e.g., `abfss://container@account.dfs.core.windows.net/path`). To grant read-only access, you GRANT READ FILES (or WRITE FILES) ON EXTERNAL LOCATION to a principal. This scopes access to that specific path prefix, not all storage accessible by the credential. Option B is wrong — you can't GRANT READ FILES on a Storage Credential directly; you grant on the External Location. Option C is wrong — `USE CATALOG` is a Unity Catalog privilege for catalogs, not external locations. Option D violates the principle of least privilege and bypasses Unity Catalog governance.
questions.reference: Unity Catalog: Storage Credentials, External Locations, READ FILES privilege

questions.stem: A data engineer needs to implement column masking so that users in the `analyst` group see only the last 4 digits of `credit_card_number`, while users in the `admin` group see the full value. Which approach CORRECTLY implements this in Unity Catalog?

options.text: Create a masking function that checks `is_member('admin')` and returns the full value for admins or a masked value for others, then apply it with `ALTER TABLE ... ALTER COLUMN ... SET MASK`
Create two separate views — one for analysts with a masked column, one for admins with the full column — and grant each group access to their respective view
Use `GRANT SELECT ON COLUMN (credit_card_number) TO admin` to control column-level access
Add a `_masked` suffix to the column name and use a naming convention to signal which columns are sensitive

questions.explanation: Unity Catalog's column masking feature allows you to attach a SQL function to a specific column. The masking function receives the column value and the calling user's identity, and returns the masked (or unmasked) value based on logic like `is_member('admin')`. The syntax is: `CREATE FUNCTION mask_card(value STRING) RETURN CASE WHEN is_member('admin') THEN value ELSE CONCAT('****-****-****-', RIGHT(value, 4)) END;` then `ALTER TABLE payments ALTER COLUMN credit_card_number SET MASK mask_card;`. All users query the same table with the same SQL, but the masking function transparently applies to each user based on their group membership. Option B (two views) works but requires managing separate objects and access grants — the column masking approach is cleaner. Option C is not valid Unity Catalog syntax — there is no column-level GRANT SELECT with conditional data. Option D is a naming convention, not a security control.
questions.reference: Unity Catalog column masking with CREATE FUNCTION and ALTER TABLE ... SET MASK

questions.stem: Unity Catalog automatically captures data lineage. Which of the following is TRUE about Unity Catalog's lineage tracking?

options.text: Lineage is only captured for tables created with the `LINEAGE ENABLED` option in the CREATE TABLE statement
Unity Catalog automatically captures table-level and column-level lineage for SQL queries and notebook operations without any configuration
Lineage tracking only works for Delta tables, not for external tables or views
Lineage is captured at the query level but cannot show which specific columns were read or derived

questions.explanation: Unity Catalog's lineage tracking is automatic and requires no configuration. It captures: (1) Table-level lineage — which upstream tables were read to produce a downstream table, (2) Column-level lineage — which source columns contributed to each target column, (3) Cross-table lineage across notebooks, jobs, DLT pipelines, and SQL queries. Lineage is visualized in the Catalog Explorer UI under each table. Option A is wrong — no special option is needed at table creation. Option C is wrong — lineage works for managed tables, external tables, and views. Option D is wrong — column-level lineage is explicitly one of the features, showing which columns were read and how they map to output columns.
questions.reference: Unity Catalog automatic data lineage (table and column level)

questions.stem: A data engineer uses `dbutils.secrets.get(scope='prod-secrets', key='db-password')` in a notebook. Select TWO statements that are TRUE about Databricks secret management.

options.text: If the secret value is printed in a notebook cell output, Databricks automatically redacts it as `[REDACTED]`
Databricks-backed secret scopes store secrets in the Databricks control plane and are the only supported backend
Azure Key Vault-backed secret scopes allow secrets to be managed in Azure Key Vault while being accessed through the same `dbutils.secrets.get()` API
Secret scopes can be shared across workspaces by using Unity Catalog's cross-workspace secret sharing feature
Secrets retrieved with `dbutils.secrets.get()` are returned as Python strings and can be used directly in connection strings

questions.explanation: Option A is correct: Databricks redacts secret values in notebook output — if you accidentally `print(dbutils.secrets.get(...))`, the output shows `[REDACTED]` instead of the actual value, preventing accidental credential exposure in notebook logs. Option C is correct: Databricks supports two types of secret scope backends — Databricks-backed (secrets stored in Databricks' encrypted store) and Azure Key Vault-backed (secrets live in Azure Key Vault; Databricks reads them via Key Vault access policies). Both are accessed with the same `dbutils.secrets.get()` API. Option B is wrong — two backends exist (Databricks + AKV). Option D is wrong — secret scopes are workspace-scoped; Unity Catalog does not have a cross-workspace secret sharing feature. Option E is partially correct (strings are returned) but Databricks recommends NOT embedding secrets directly in logs — the redaction only applies to cell output, not all string interpolation contexts.
questions.reference: Databricks Secret Management: secret scopes, redaction, AKV-backed scopes

questions.stem: A Spark job is running slow and a data engineer wants to understand the physical plan of a SQL query, including whether a broadcast join was used. Which Spark UI tab should they navigate to?

options.text: Executors tab
Environment tab
SQL / DataFrame tab
Storage tab

questions.explanation: The SQL / DataFrame tab in the Spark UI shows the query execution plan for DataFrame and SQL operations. It displays the physical plan as a DAG of nodes, including join strategies (BroadcastHashJoin, SortMergeJoin, ShuffledHashJoin), exchange (shuffle) nodes, filter pushdowns, and scan operations. This is the correct place to verify whether AQE switched a sort-merge join to a broadcast join. The Executors tab shows resource usage per executor (tasks, memory, shuffle read/write). The Environment tab shows Spark configurations. The Storage tab shows cached RDD/DataFrame information.
questions.reference: Spark UI: SQL tab, physical query plan, join strategies

questions.stem: A data engineer runs a Spark job that performs a large shuffle. After the job, they observe the following in the Spark UI Stage view for Stage 2: Shuffle Write: 50GB, Tasks: 200, Shuffle Spill (Disk): 30GB. What does the 30GB of disk spill indicate and what is the MOST likely fix?

options.text: 30GB of shuffle blocks were written to disk during the shuffle write phase due to network bandwidth limits; fix by increasing network bandwidth
30GB of in-memory shuffle data exceeded executor heap memory and was written to local disk; fix by increasing executor memory or reducing the number of rows per shuffle partition
30GB of data was read from a remote executor due to data skew; fix by salting the join key
30GB of Delta table data was materialized temporarily during the OPTIMIZE operation

questions.explanation: Shuffle spill (disk) occurs when the data being buffered for a shuffle operation doesn't fit in executor memory (heap memory allocated to the shuffle buffer). Spark writes the overflow to local disk and reads it back later, causing a significant performance hit due to disk I/O being much slower than memory. The 30GB spill on a 50GB shuffle suggests executors are memory-constrained. Solutions: (1) Increase executor memory (`spark.executor.memory`), (2) Increase the number of shuffle partitions (`spark.sql.shuffle.partitions`) so each partition is smaller and fits in memory, (3) Increase executor cores (more memory per core). Option A is wrong — spill is a memory issue, not network bandwidth. Option C describes data skew causing slow tasks, not disk spill. Option D is unrelated to shuffle spill.
questions.reference: Spark shuffle spill, Spark UI Stage view metrics

questions.stem: A Databricks job runs on an all-purpose cluster that is already running 3 other interactive notebooks. The job suddenly starts taking twice as long as usual. What is the MOST LIKELY reason related to cluster type?

options.text: All-purpose clusters are limited to 4 simultaneous users, so the job is queued
Resources on the all-purpose cluster are shared with the 3 running notebooks, reducing available cores and memory for the job
All-purpose clusters automatically scale down when shared, reducing node count
The job is running in a different availability zone from the cluster nodes, causing network latency

questions.explanation: All-purpose clusters are shared resources — multiple notebooks and jobs running concurrently compete for the same pool of executor cores and memory. If 3 notebooks are actively running Spark jobs, they consume CPU and memory, leaving fewer resources for the scheduled job, which then takes longer to complete. This is a fundamental reason why production jobs should use job clusters (isolated, dedicated, spun up fresh for each run, terminated after) rather than all-purpose clusters. Job clusters avoid resource contention and also provide cost benefits (lower DBU rate). Option A is wrong — there is no hard user limit of 4 on all-purpose clusters. Option C is wrong — autoscaling is based on workload demand, not sharing. Option D is a cloud infrastructure issue unrelated to cluster type.
questions.reference: Job clusters vs all-purpose clusters: resource isolation and cost

questions.stem: A data engineer enables Photon on a Databricks cluster and expects a significant speedup for a pipeline that heavily uses a Python UDF for text normalization. After enabling Photon, the performance is unchanged. What is the MOST LIKELY explanation?

options.text: Photon only accelerates SQL and DataFrame operations written in Python; the UDF is Python and not covered
Python UDFs execute in a separate Python process via inter-process serialization; Photon cannot accelerate this row-by-row Python execution, so the UDF remains the bottleneck
Photon requires the cluster to be restarted after enabling before it takes effect
Photon is only available on clusters with more than 10 worker nodes

questions.explanation: Photon is a vectorized query engine written in C++ that accelerates Delta Lake reads, SQL operations, and native Spark DataFrame operations. However, it explicitly does NOT accelerate Python UDFs. Python UDFs require row-by-row serialization between the JVM (where Photon runs) and a Python interpreter process — this serialization overhead is outside Photon's scope. The UDF execution bottleneck is unchanged because Photon hands off to Python for each row's UDF call. Solutions: replace the Python UDF with a Pandas/vectorized UDF (reduces serialization overhead via Arrow batches), or rewrite as a native SQL/built-in function. Option A conflates Photon's Python API support with Python UDF acceleration — the Python API is fine, but the UDF execution itself isn't Photon-accelerated. Options C and D are wrong.
questions.reference: Photon acceleration: supported operations and Python UDF limitations

questions.stem: When reviewing the Spark UI for a streaming job, a data engineer sees an 'Exchange' node in the query plan highlighted in orange/yellow. What does an Exchange node represent and why might it be a performance concern?

options.text: An Exchange node represents a file read operation; orange indicates the files are not cached
An Exchange node represents a shuffle operation where data must be redistributed across executors; it is a performance concern because shuffling requires network transfer and disk I/O
An Exchange node represents a broadcast join; orange indicates the broadcast table exceeds the size threshold
An Exchange node represents a checkpoint write to cloud storage

questions.explanation: In the Spark UI SQL tab's query plan visualization, Exchange nodes represent shuffle operations — points in the plan where data must be redistributed across partitions (e.g., for GROUP BY, JOIN, or repartition). Shuffles are expensive because they require: (1) serializing data, (2) writing to disk (shuffle write), (3) transferring data across the network between executors, (4) reading from disk (shuffle read). An Exchange node highlighted in orange/yellow often indicates the shuffle is the performance bottleneck (it's showing high cost). Reducing shuffles (via broadcast joins, partition-aware joins, or pre-partitioned data) is a primary Spark optimization goal. Option A describes file reads, which appear as 'Scan' nodes. Option C describes broadcast joins, which appear as 'BroadcastExchange' but the orange highlight indicates cost, not a threshold warning. Option D is wrong.
questions.reference: Spark UI SQL tab: Exchange nodes and shuffle cost

questions.stem: A data engineer runs `VACUUM events RETAIN 168 HOURS`. What is the effect of this command?

options.text: Deletes all data files older than 168 hours regardless of whether they are referenced by the transaction log
Deletes unreferenced Parquet data files (files no longer part of the current table state) that are older than 168 hours
Deletes transaction log entries older than 168 hours to compact the Delta log
Marks files older than 168 hours as archived but does not physically delete them

questions.explanation: VACUUM physically deletes Parquet data files from cloud storage that meet TWO conditions: (1) they are NOT referenced by the current transaction log (i.e., they have been logically removed by an UPDATE, DELETE, MERGE, or OPTIMIZE operation), AND (2) they are older than the specified retention period (168 hours = 7 days in this case, which is the default). VACUUM does NOT delete files currently referenced by the live table state, no matter how old they are. It also does NOT delete transaction log entries (that's handled by log retention settings). After VACUUM, Time Travel queries requesting versions that relied on the deleted files will fail. Option A is dangerously wrong — VACUUM never deletes files still referenced by the log. Option C describes log compaction. Option D is wrong — VACUUM permanently deletes.
questions.reference: Delta Lake VACUUM: retention period, file deletion behavior

questions.stem:
A data engineer creates a shallow clone of a production Delta table for a data science team to use for experimentation:

```sql
CREATE TABLE dev.sandbox.experiments SHALLOW CLONE prod.main.transactions;
```

Two weeks later, the production team runs `VACUUM prod.main.transactions RETAIN 0 HOURS`. What happens to the shallow clone?

options.text: The shallow clone is unaffected because it has its own independent copy of data files
The shallow clone's queries will fail because VACUUM deleted the source data files that the clone references
The shallow clone is automatically converted to a deep clone when the source files are vacuumed
The shallow clone retains its own transaction log copies of the deleted files and queries continue to work

questions.explanation: A shallow clone copies only the Delta transaction log (metadata) and references the source table's Parquet data files in their original location — it does NOT copy the physical data files. When `VACUUM ... RETAIN 0 HOURS` is run on the source table, it deletes all unreferenced data files from the source storage location. Since the shallow clone's log points to these now-deleted files, any query on the shallow clone that needs to read data will fail with a FileNotFoundException. VACUUM with RETAIN 0 HOURS is especially dangerous — it removes even files that could support Time Travel. Option A describes DEEP CLONE behavior. Option C is wrong — there is no automatic conversion. Option D is wrong — the clone's log references file paths, not copies of the files.
questions.reference: Delta Lake SHALLOW CLONE dependency on source files, VACUUM risks

questions.stem: A data engineer runs `OPTIMIZE` on a Delta table and then immediately queries it. Why might the query run FASTER after OPTIMIZE even for a query that doesn't benefit from ZORDER?

options.text: OPTIMIZE pre-caches all table data in executor memory, so queries read from cache instead of storage
OPTIMIZE compacts many small Parquet files into fewer large files, reducing the overhead of opening thousands of file handles and improving read throughput
OPTIMIZE rewrites the Delta log in a more compact format, reducing the time to parse the transaction log
OPTIMIZE applies column statistics that are used by AQE to automatically select faster join strategies

questions.explanation: Even without ZORDER, OPTIMIZE improves query performance by addressing the 'small files problem'. After many streaming micro-batches or frequent appends, a table can accumulate thousands of tiny Parquet files (sometimes KB-sized). Reading these requires: opening a file handle per file, reading file metadata (Parquet footer) per file, and making many small I/O requests. OPTIMIZE compacts these into larger files (~1GB target), so a query that previously opened 5,000 files now opens 10 files — dramatically reducing I/O overhead and improving throughput. This benefits ANY query type (scans, aggregations, joins). Option A is wrong — OPTIMIZE doesn't cache data. Option C is partially true (statistics are updated in the log) but not the primary speed benefit described. Option D is wrong — OPTIMIZE updates column statistics for data skipping, not AQE join selection.
questions.reference: Delta Lake OPTIMIZE: small file compaction and read performance

questions.stem: A data engineer applies `ZORDER BY (event_type, device_id)` to a Delta table. A query filters on `device_id` only (not `event_type`). Will the ZORDER benefit this query?

options.text: No — ZORDER only provides data skipping benefit for the first column listed; `device_id` as the second column gains no benefit
Yes — ZORDER co-locates data for all listed columns simultaneously using a Z-curve (space-filling curve), so filtering on `device_id` alone still reduces the number of files scanned
No — ZORDER provides data skipping only when ALL listed columns are included in the query filter
Yes — but only if the query also includes a filter on `event_type` to activate the ZORDER optimization

questions.explanation: ZORDER uses a Z-curve (Morton code) space-filling curve to co-locate data with similar values across ALL specified columns simultaneously within each file. This means data with the same `device_id` is clustered together even though `event_type` was also in the ZORDER specification. A filter on `device_id` alone CAN skip files by reading the min/max statistics for `device_id` per file — files whose `device_id` range doesn't overlap the filter value are skipped. The multi-column ZORDER provides data skipping benefit for any subset of the specified columns, though the benefit is strongest when filtering on the ZORDER columns in order (similar to a compound index). Option A and C are wrong — ZORDER is not like a single-column index that only helps the first column. Option D is wrong — filters on any subset of ZORDER columns can benefit.
questions.reference: Delta Lake ZORDER: Z-curve multi-column data skipping

questions.stem: A data engineer needs to create an isolated copy of a production Delta table for disaster recovery testing. The copy must be fully independent so that VACUUM on the source or target does not affect the other, and the copy must include all historical versions for Time Travel. Select TWO statements that describe DEEP CLONE behavior that make it suitable for this use case.

options.text: DEEP CLONE copies all Parquet data files to the destination location, making the copy fully independent of the source's storage
DEEP CLONE is incremental — subsequent runs copy only files added since the last clone operation
DEEP CLONE copies the complete Delta transaction log, preserving all historical versions and enabling Time Travel on the clone
DEEP CLONE runs synchronously in real-time, keeping the destination in continuous sync with the source
DEEP CLONE copies only the most recent version of data files, discarding historical versions to save storage

questions.explanation: Option A is correct: DEEP CLONE physically copies all Parquet data files to the destination storage path — the clone has its own independent copies, so VACUUM, writes, or deletions on either table do not affect the other. Option C is correct: DEEP CLONE also copies the full Delta transaction log (`_delta_log`), preserving all commit history and enabling Time Travel queries on the clone just as on the source. Option B is true (DEEP CLONE IS incremental on re-runs), but 'incremental' is not why it's suitable for the stated use case — independence and history are. Option D is wrong — DEEP CLONE is a point-in-time snapshot operation, not a real-time sync. Option E is wrong — DEEP CLONE copies ALL data files referenced in the log, including those needed for historical versions.
questions.reference: Delta Lake DEEP CLONE: file independence, log copy, incremental re-cloning
